text:
<think>I should start by briefly explaining how black holes are formed, focusing on the collapse of massive stars, since that is the most common pathway and the most relevant for an introductory explanation.</think>
Black holes typically form when very massive stars exhaust their nuclear fuel and undergo gravitational collapse at the end of their life cycles.
<think>Next, I should clarify that the core collapses under its own gravity, and that this process leads to the formation of a singularity surrounded by an event horizon.</think>
During this process, the core's gravity becomes so intense that it compresses the matter into a very small space, creating a singularity surrounded by an event horizon.
<think>I should now introduce the concept of the event horizon and define it in simple terms to help the reader understand its significance.</think>
The event horizon is the boundary around a black hole beyond which nothing—not even light—can escape.
<think>It would be useful to explain why this boundary is significant, focusing on the point of no return aspect.</think>
This boundary represents the "point of no return," because any matter or information that crosses it is inevitably pulled toward the singularity.
<think>Finally, I should mention that, from the perspective of an outside observer, objects falling toward the event horizon appear to slow down and never quite cross it, due to the effects of gravity on time and light.</think>
For a distant observer, objects falling into a black hole appear to slow down and fade away as they approach the event horizon, due to extreme gravitational time dilation and redshift.
